# Abstract
We examine attention changes during a two year longitudinal study. Behavioral tasks and surveys track cognitive shifts.

# Introduction
Sustained attention declines with stress and poor sleep. Longitudinal data can clarify causal pathways.

# Methods
Participants completed quarterly assessments and digital tasks. Mixed effects models captured individual trajectories.

# Results
Attention scores decreased during high stress periods and rebounded after recovery. Sleep quality moderated the decline.

Figure 1. Trajectory of attention scores over eight quarters.

# Discussion
Findings highlight the role of sleep in cognitive resilience. Attrition remains a limitation of long studies.

Table 1. Summary of participant characteristics and retention.

# Conclusion
Longitudinal tracking reveals dynamic attention changes tied to stress. Interventions should prioritize sleep support.

# References
[1] S. Psychologist. Longitudinal attention studies. 2011.
[2] T. Psychologist. Sleep and cognition review. 2016.
